viewing-file-contents.label: Viewing File Contents
viewing-file-contents.summary: Read text files with cat, preview long files with head and tail, and count lines with wc -l.

cat-whole-file.title: Use `cat` for a small file
cat-whole-file.bodyMarkdown:
`cat` prints an entire text file to the terminal. It is a good choice for a short recipe, note, or small configuration file.

```bash
cat recipe-card.txt
```

Reading with `cat` does not modify the file. Check the file name carefully: if the path is wrong, the command reports that the file does not exist. For a long log, printing everything can bury the useful lines, so the next sketch introduces smaller previews.

head-tail-preview.title: Use `head` and `tail` for previews
head-tail-preview.bodyMarkdown:
`head` shows the beginning of a file, while `tail` shows the end. They are useful when a long file has a meaningful header or the newest entries appear last.

```bash
head sensor-log.txt
tail sensor-log.txt
```

Both commands inspect without changing content. Keep the file path the same when comparing the first and last lines; otherwise you may believe one file is inconsistent when you actually opened two different files. Use a preview first, then `cat` only when the whole file is short enough to read.

wc-line-count.title: Use `wc -l` to count lines
wc-line-count.bodyMarkdown:
`wc -l` counts the lines in a text file. It gives a quick size check for lists, logs, and line-based records.

```bash
wc -l reading-list.txt
```

The output includes a number and usually the file name. A line count does not prove the content is correct, but it can reveal a missing or duplicated record before a handoff. Combine it with `head`, `tail`, or `cat` when you need both a quantity and a content check.
